LOOK.text: Below, in smaller print: 'Accepting patients 24/7. No appointment needed. Your wallet is your referral.'

USE.text: It's a sign, not a terminal.

TALK.text: The sign doesn't respond. Like customer support during a rug pull.

LOOK.text: Heavy reinforced doors with a biometric scanner. Built to keep patients in, not visitors out.

then.text: Keycard accepted. The scanner turns green.
The scanner reads the sign-in sheet's barcode. Visitor access granted.

else.text: The scanner blinks: 'PROOF OF STAKE REQUIRED'. I need some form of identification or authorization.
Locked. The biometric scanner needs something from me first.

TALK.text: An intercom crackles: 'Visiting hours are... always. Please authenticate.'

PUSH.text: Reinforced steel. This door doesn't budge.

PULL.text: I pull the handle. The scanner beeps angrily.

LOOK.text: A weathered drop box labeled 'VISITOR SIGN-IN'. There's a clipboard inside.

else.text: A sign-in sheet with a barcode at the bottom. Names on the list: 'V. Buterin (discharged)', 'S. Bankman (transferred)', 'D. Kwon (escaped)'.
At the very bottom: 'Patient Zero — [REDACTED]'. The barcode might work on the scanner.

OPEN.text: I open the drop box lid. The sign-in sheet is right there.

USE.text: It's a drop box. I should check what's inside.

LOOK.text: Brutalist architecture. Concrete and steel. The kind of building where hope goes to get a reality check.
No windows on the ground floor. Privacy for the patients... or containment?

TALK.text: The building absorbs sound. Designed for isolation.

LOOK.text: A camera tracks my movement. Red LED blinks steadily. Someone is watching.
Or something. Automated surveillance. On-chain, everything is watched.

TALK.text: 'Smile, you're on-chain.' The camera doesn't laugh.

USE.text: I wave at the camera. The LED blinks faster for a moment.

LOOK.text: A bench for visitors. Carved into the armrest: 'WAGMI' crossed out, replaced with 'NGMI', crossed out, replaced with '???'.

USE.text: I sit for a moment. The mountain air is cold but the bench is colder.
Someone scratched a wallet address into the seat. Too worn to read. A ghost of a transaction.

TALK.text: The bench has heard every sad portfolio story. It's not impressed by mine.

LOOK.text: "SERENITY PRAYER: Grant me serenity to accept trades I cannot reverse, courage to HODL the ones I can, and wisdom to know the difference."
"12 STEPS TO RECOVERY: Step 1 — Disconnect your wallet. Steps 2-12 — See Step 1."

USE.text: The posters are laminated. Professionally done. This clinic has budget.

LOOK.text: A metal trash can. Overflowing with crumpled papers, empty energy drink cans, and what appears to be a torn-up whitepaper titled 'SafeMoon v3.'

USE.text: I dig through the trash. Among the garbage: a receipt for '500 ETH worth of JPEGs' dated 2021. Yikes.

TALK.text: I peer into the trash can. It stares back. We are kindred spirits — both full of other people's bad decisions.

PICK.text: I fish out a crumpled note: 'Day 47: Still believe in the tech.' Heartbreaking.

OPEN.text: I lift the lid. The smell hits like a failed rebase — unexpected and overwhelming.

CLOSE.text: I slam the lid shut. Some portfolios are better left uninspected.

PUSH.text: I push the trash can. It scrapes across concrete. The sound of metal on stone — or paper hands on sell buttons.

PULL.text: I pull the trash can closer. Why am I investigating garbage? Because every good detective checks the bins.

GIVE.text: The trash can accepts everything. No KYC, no slippage, no minimum deposit. The most inclusive protocol I've ever seen.

LOOK.text: Someone spray-painted 'Gwei' on the wall near the entrance, and 'Degen' further down. The patients were tagging the clinic. Proof-of-vandalism.

USE.text: I trace the letters. Still fresh. Whoever tagged this was here recently. And clearly not rehabilitated.

TALK.text: I read the graffiti aloud: 'Gwei.' It's a unit of gas, a cry for help, and a tag — all at once.

PICK.text: I can't peel graffiti off concrete. It's as permanent as a transaction on the blockchain. No rollbacks.

OPEN.text: There's nothing to open. Graffiti is already the most open form of expression.

CLOSE.text: I can't close graffiti. It's open-source art. Permissionless and uncensorable.

PUSH.text: I push against the tagged wall. The paint doesn't smudge. Committed to the surface forever.

PULL.text: I scratch at the graffiti. Under 'Gwei' someone wrote 'was here' in pencil. Under THAT: 'we are all here.'

GIVE.text: The graffiti has already been given to the world. A public good, like Ethereum. Or litter. Depends on your perspective.

LOOK.text: A cracked concrete walkway leading to the clinic entrance. Weeds push through the seams. Nature's own fork of the pavement.

USE.text: I follow the path toward the entrance. Each crack in the pavement feels intentional, like breadcrumbs for the lost.

TALK.text: My footsteps echo on the walkway. The path has heard a thousand desperate shuffles toward recovery.

PICK.text: I pick up a pebble from a crack. It's been here longer than most crypto projects will ever last.

OPEN.text: The path is already open. It's always been open. The hard part is choosing to walk it.

CLOSE.text: I can't close a path. That's the urban planning department's job. And they don't do weekends.

PUSH.text: I push forward on the path. Each step closer to the truth about Patient Zero.

PULL.text: Something about this place pulls me forward and pushes me back at the same time. Like a market at equilibrium.

GIVE.text: The path gives direction. That's more than any financial advisor ever gave me.
